questions.q: What is depreciation, and how is it calculated? Explain the different methods of depreciation with suitable examples. (a) Straight Line Method, (b) Written Down Value Method, (c) Diminishing Balance Method. (5 marks)
questions.answer: Depreciation is a decrease in the value of an asset over time due to wear and tear, obsolescence, or other factors. It is calculated by subtracting the residual value of the asset from its cost price. The three methods of depreciation are: (a) Straight Line Method (SLM): This method assumes that the asset loses its value at a constant rate over its useful life. The formula for SLM is: Depreciation = (Cost Price - Residual Value) / Useful Life. For example, an asset costing Rs. 10,000 has a residual value of Rs. 2,000 and a useful life of 5 years. The depreciation per year would be Rs. 1,600 (Rs. 8,000 / 5 years). (b) Written Down Value Method (WDV): This method assumes that the asset loses its value at a decreasing rate over its useful life. The formula for WDV is: Depreciation = (Cost Price - Accumulated Depreciation) / (1 - Residual Value). For example, an asset costing Rs. 10,000 has a residual value of Rs. 2,000 and a useful life of 5 years. The depreciation per year would be Rs. 1,600 (Rs. 8,000 / 5 years). (c) Diminishing Balance Method (DBM): This method assumes that the asset loses its value at a rate that decreases over its useful life. The formula for DBM is: Depreciation = (Accumulated Depreciation x Rate) / 100. For example, an asset costing Rs. 10,000 has a residual value of Rs. 2,000 and a useful life of 5 years. The depreciation per year would be Rs. 1,600 (Rs. 8,000 / 5 years).
marking_scheme: Explain the concept of depreciation and its importance in accounting. (1 mark)
Mention the three methods of depreciation with suitable examples. (2 marks)
Calculate the depreciation for each method using the given examples. (2 marks)

questions.q: What is a provision, and how is it created? Explain the different types of provisions with suitable examples. (a) Contingent Provision, (b) Accrued Provision, (c) Deferred Revenue Expenditure. (5 marks)
questions.answer: A provision is a liability that is expected to be incurred or paid out in the future. It is created when a company expects to incur a future expense or liability. The three types of provisions are: (a) Contingent Provision: This provision is created when a company expects to incur a future expense or liability that is uncertain or contingent. For example, a company may have a contingent provision for a law suit that may be filed against it. (b) Accrued Provision: This provision is created when a company expects to incur a future expense or liability that is certain but not yet incurred. For example, a company may have an accrued provision for salaries payable to its employees. (c) Deferred Revenue Expenditure: This provision is created when a company incurs an expense that will be recovered in future. For example, a company may have a deferred revenue expenditure provision for a prepaid insurance premium.
marking_scheme: Explain the concept of provision and its importance in accounting. (1 mark)
Mention the three types of provisions with suitable examples. (2 marks)
Explain the accounting treatment for each type of provision. (2 marks)

questions.q: Explain the concept of reserve and its importance in accounting. What are the different types of reserves? (a) Profit and Loss Reserve, (b) General Reserve, (c) Capital Reserve. (5 marks)
questions.answer: A reserve is an amount set aside from profits or other sources to meet future expenses or liabilities. It is an important concept in accounting as it helps to maintain the liquidity and solvency of a company. The three types of reserves are: (a) Profit and Loss Reserve: This reserve is created when a company makes a profit or incurs a loss. It is used to meet future expenses or liabilities. For example, a company may have a profit and loss reserve of Rs. 10,000 to meet future expenses. (b) General Reserve: This reserve is created when a company has a surplus of profits. It is used to meet future expenses or liabilities. For example, a company may have a general reserve of Rs. 20,000 to meet future expenses. (c) Capital Reserve: This reserve is created when a company receives an amount in excess of its share capital. It is used to meet future expenses or liabilities. For example, a company may have a capital reserve of Rs. 30,000 to meet future expenses.
marking_scheme: Explain the concept of reserve and its importance in accounting. (1 mark)
Mention the three types of reserves with suitable examples. (2 marks)
Explain the accounting treatment for each type of reserve. (2 marks)

questions.q: A company has an asset costing Rs. 10,000 with a useful life of 5 years and a residual value of Rs. 2,000. Calculate the depreciation for the first year using the Straight Line Method (SLM) and the Diminishing Balance Method (DBM). (5 marks)
questions.answer: To calculate the depreciation using the SLM, we use the formula: Depreciation = (Cost Price - Residual Value) / Useful Life. Substituting the values, we get: Depreciation = (Rs. 10,000 - Rs. 2,000) / 5 = Rs. 1,600. To calculate the depreciation using the DBM, we use the formula: Depreciation = (Accumulated Depreciation x Rate) / 100. The rate is calculated as: Rate = (Cost Price - Residual Value) / (Useful Life x Cost Price). Substituting the values, we get: Rate = (Rs. 10,000 - Rs. 2,000) / (5 x Rs. 10,000) = 1.8%. The depreciation for the first year is: Depreciation = (Accumulated Depreciation x Rate) / 100 = (Rs. 0 x 1.8%) = Rs. 0.
marking_scheme: Calculate the depreciation using the SLM. (2 marks)
Calculate the depreciation using the DBM. (2 marks)
Compare the depreciation calculated using both methods. (1 mark)

questions.q: A company has a contingent liability of Rs. 10,000. Explain how the company would account for this liability if it is probable that the liability will be incurred but not yet certain. (5 marks)
questions.answer: If the company expects to incur a contingent liability of Rs. 10,000, it would create a contingent provision in the balance sheet. The contingent provision would be shown as a liability and would be measured at the best estimate of the amount that will be incurred. The company would need to disclose the contingent provision in the notes to the financial statements and would also need to provide a commentary on the nature of the liability and the amount that is expected to be incurred. The accounting treatment for a contingent liability is as follows: (a) If the contingency is probable, the company would recognize a liability and provide for the expected cost. (b) If the contingency is possible but not probable, the company would disclose the contingency in the notes to the financial statements but would not recognize a liability. (c) If the contingency is remote, the company would not recognize a liability or disclose the contingency in the notes to the financial statements.
marking_scheme: Explain the accounting treatment for a contingent liability. (2 marks)
Discuss the factors that affect the accounting treatment for a contingent liability. (2 marks)
Explain how the company would disclose the contingent liability in the financial statements. (1 mark)
